Q1: I organized my study routine around my work schedule and other responsibilities, which meant I had to be really intentional about carving out time. I usually dedicated about 23 hours a week to studying, spread across evenings and weekends. I'd make lists of what needed to be done and prioritize assignments that were due soonest. Since I'm a read-write learner, I spent a lot of time reading through materials and writing detailed notes to help things stick. It wasn't always perfect, but having some structure kept me from falling too far behind.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was happening in my life and when deadlines were approaching. Some weeks I could stick to a more regular schedule, but other times work or personal stuff would throw everything off. When big assignments were due, I'd shift into a more intense mode and study longer hours in the days leading up to the deadline. I tried to keep some consistency, but honestly, flexibility was key to surviving with limited resources and time.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS to check for new announcements, see if grades were posted, or access materials for whatever I was working on. Sometimes I'd get a notification reminder that would prompt me to log in. I also made it a habit to check in before starting any study session so I knew exactly what was expected and didn't miss anything important. It became kind of like checking my email—something I did regularly to stay on top of things.
Q4: Yes, after a few weeks, logging into the LMS became pretty automatic for me. I think it happened because I realized that missing updates could really hurt my progress, so I built it into my daily routine. Every morning with my coffee, I'd do a quick check of the platform before heading to work. The peer support group I connected with also kept me accountable—we'd remind each other about things posted on the LMS, which reinforced the habit.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were definitely the most useful for me since that's how I learn best. I also found the discussion forums helpful because I could read through other students' questions and explanations, which sometimes clarified things I was confused about. The assignment submission portal with clear rubrics was great too—it helped me understand exactly what was expected. Videos were okay, but I honestly preferred reading the transcripts when available.
Q6: I often skipped or minimally used the video content because watching 20 minutes of video felt like a lot when I could read the same information faster. Some of the supplementary resources that weren't directly tied to assignments also got ignored because I had to prioritize with my limited time. I wasn't trying to be dismissive of those resources, but when you're juggling a lot and have low resources to work with, you have to be strategic about where you put your energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could clearly see how it connected to my assignments or exams. If I could tell it would help me understand a concept better or directly improve my grade, I was all in. Practical applications and examples made things feel relevant rather than abstract. I also valued activities that I could complete efficiently—I didn't have the luxury of spending hours on something that only marginally helped my learning.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because there was no immediate consequence for skipping them. With required activities, there was this sense of urgency and accountability that pushed me to complete them even when I was tired. Optional ones felt more like nice-to-haves, and honestly, with everything else on my plate, they often got pushed aside. Sometimes I'd feel guilty about not doing them, but I had to accept that I couldn't do everything.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I would download readings and PDFs to my phone so I could study during my commute or breaks at work. I also kept a physical notebook where I wrote out summaries and key concepts—that's really how I process information best. Sometimes I'd borrow textbooks from the library or find free resources online that supplemented what was in the LMS. That offline work was a huge part of my learning.
Q10: There were many times when I was deeply engaged with the material but it wouldn't show up in the system at all. For example, I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes or re-reading downloaded PDFs without being logged in. Study sessions with my peer support group happened over messaging apps, not on the LMS. I'd also quiz myself using flashcards I made, which was really effective but completely invisible to any tracking system.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main approach. I'd download materials during one session, then spend several days working through them offline. With my internet access being decent but my time being limited, it made more sense to grab everything at once and study when I could. I'd read through documents multiple times, annotate them, and create study guides—all without touching the LMS again until I needed something new.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, usually when work got really demanding or when I felt overwhelmed by the volume of coursework across all eleven courses. Mid-semester was particularly tough when multiple assignments piled up at once. During those times, I had to make hard choices about what to prioritize. The peer support really helped me push through those moments though—knowing others were struggling too made it feel more manageable.
Q13: Yes, all of those factors affected my LMS use at different points. When I was fatigued from work, I'd do the bare minimum on the platform—just check what was due and grab what I needed. Confusion sometimes made me avoid certain modules temporarily because I didn't have the mental energy to tackle them. Competing responsibilities meant I couldn't always engage as deeply as I wanted to, so I'd skim materials instead of reading thoroughly.
Q14: Honestly, there were times when I'd open materials and my mind would just wander because I was too tired or stressed. I'd be scrolling through readings but not really absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd watch a video and realize at the end that I hadn't retained much. Those moments were frustrating because I knew I was putting in time, but the quality of my engagement wasn't there. I'd usually have to come back later when I was more focused.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts sometimes because they required more mental energy than just reading or completing straightforward assignments. Even though I knew participating would help my understanding, I'd push it off when I was overwhelmed. I also delayed starting larger assignments because they felt intimidating, which sometimes hurt my completion rate. It wasn't laziness—it was more about managing my limited capacity across too many demands.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me. My LMS activity would spike noticeably in the days before something was due. I'd log in more frequently, access materials multiple times, and participate more in discussions when I knew it counted toward my grade. Without deadlines, I think I would have struggled to maintain any momentum. They gave structure to my otherwise chaotic schedule and helped me prioritize what needed attention first.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I received encouraging comments or saw that my efforts were recognized, I felt more motivated to keep pushing. Constructive criticism helped me understand where to focus my energy for improvement. However, when feedback was delayed, I sometimes felt disconnected from the course because I didn't know if I was on the right track. Quick feedback loops kept me more engaged overall.
Q18: The course structure had a big impact on my participation. When modules were clearly organized with explicit expectations, I could plan my time effectively and stay on track. The weekly rhythm helped me build habits around when to study and what to prioritize. However, when eleven courses all had different structures, it got confusing and I sometimes missed things. Consistency in how information was presented made a real difference for me.
Q19: Clear rubrics and well-organized content made engagement much easier—I knew exactly what was expected and could plan accordingly. Having materials available for download was crucial for my offline studying. What made it harder was when important information was buried in multiple clicks or when there were too many different tools to navigate. Sometimes the sheer volume of content across all my courses felt overwhelming and made it hard to know where to start.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling to produce quality work. They might be reading everything, watching all the videos, participating in discussions, but then hit a wall when it comes to actually completing assignments. It could also be anxiety about submitting imperfect work, or life circumstances that interrupt their ability to finish what they started. High activity doesn't always translate to completed outputs.
Q21: That definitely reflects part of my experience. I learned a lot through offline methods—reading downloaded materials, writing notes by hand, studying with peers outside the platform. My exam score and assignment scores show I understood the material reasonably well, even though my LMS activity was just moderate. Some students are efficient learners who don't need to spend hours on the platform to grasp concepts. The LMS is just one piece of the learning puzzle.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active, how often you access materials, and whether you're meeting deadlines. That's useful for identifying students who might be falling behind. But they miss so much—the quality of engagement, offline learning, peer discussions, and the mental and emotional effort that goes into studying. They can't capture whether someone is truly understanding the material or just clicking through. Numbers don't tell the whole story.
Q23: To me, being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions to get a grade. It's about being curious, asking questions, connecting concepts to real life, and putting in effort even when it's hard. It's also about feeling connected to the course community—peers and instructors. Engagement isn't just about time spent; it's about the quality of attention and the intention behind your actions.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much I relied on my peer support network for motivation and understanding—those conversations happened outside the platform but were essential to my success. It wouldn't capture my offline studying, my handwritten notes, or the hours I spent reviewing materials on my phone during commutes. It also can't show my emotional journey—the moments of doubt I pushed through, or the satisfaction when concepts finally clicked. My resilience and determination don't show up in login counts.
